title: Hierarchical Feature Maps
concept: Swin Transformer uses patch merging to gradually downsample feature maps, creating a hierarchical pyramid representation suitable for multi-scale dense predictions.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main role of shifted windows in Swin?

options.text: To allow communication across adjacent local window boundaries.
To speed up the MLP layers.

exercises.explain: Correct! Shifting grid lines mixes representations across window boundaries.

cards.title: The feature pyramid
cards.body: Standard ViT keeps the same number of tokens from start to finish. But CNNs get their power by gradually pooling features, creating a pyramid: sharp details early, broad concepts late. Can we build a transformer that does the same thing? Yes, using patch merging!

cards.title: Hierarchical Pyramids
cards.body: Swin Transformer constructs hierarchical feature maps. It starts with small patches and gradually groups adjacent patches together in deeper layers using a patch merging layer. This produces feature maps of resolutions H/4, H/8, H/16, and H/32, mirroring ResNet structures.

cards.title: Google Maps zoom levels
cards.body: Imagine zooming out on Google Maps. In stage 1 (close-up), you see individual houses (pixels). In stage 2, you group houses into neighborhoods (H/8). In stage 3, neighborhoods become cities (H/16). In stage 4 (zoomed out), you just see states (H/32). Swin does this zoom-out step systematically.

cards.title: Patch Merging Layer
cards.body: To downsample by 2x, patch merging concatenates the features of each 2x2 group of neighboring patches (turning 4 tokens into 1 token with 4x channels), and then applies a linear layer to reduce the channel dimension by half.

cards.title: Not the same as pooling
cards.body: You might think patch merging is just max pooling. Unlike pooling which discards information to shrink size, patch merging **concatenates all features** first, allowing the linear layer to decide how to combine the channel information without losing details.

left.text:
Token count constant (e.g. 196)
Resolution stays H/16 throughout
Struggles with multi-scale objects

right.text:
Token count shrinks: H/4 -> H/32
Resolution matches ResNet stages
Excellent for multi-scale objects

exercises.prompt: How does Swin Transformer downsample feature maps in deeper layers?
exercises.explain: Patch merging concatenates 2x2 groups of patches and applies a linear layer to reduce spatial dimensions while doubling channel depth.

options.text: Using patch merging layers to group neighboring patches and project them
By deleting half the rows of the image
By scaling down the projection weight values
Using a fixed-size Gaussian blur kernel

exercises.prompt: True or false: Standard ViT maintains a constant sequence length, whereas Swin's sequence length shrinks as it gets deeper.
exercises.explain: True. Swin downsamples features at each stage, reducing token count to form a hierarchical representation.
U-Net and FPN rely on hierarchical feature grids to detect objects at different scales, which Swin fits perfectly.
exercises.sentence: Swin's multi-resolution pyramid design makes it easy to integrate with architectures like FPN and ___ for object detection.

exercises.prompt: Which of these describes the outputs of the four stages in a Swin Transformer?
exercises.explain: Swin's stages progressively reduce resolution from H/4 down to H/32, creating the hierarchical pyramid.

options.text: Feature maps at H/4, H/8, H/16, and H/32 resolutions
Four identical copies of the classification token
A list of bounding box coordinates

exercises.prompt: Why is a hierarchical feature map layout superior for object detection tasks?

options.text: Because it allows the model to detect large objects in the low-resolution stages and tiny objects in the high-resolution stages.
Because it prevents the model from using memory.
Because it forces all bounding boxes to be the same size.
Because it removes the need for self-attention.

exercises.explain: Superb! Multi-scale features allow the detector to capture objects of all sizes, from a tiny bird in the distance to a massive truck nearby. Next: Swin in practice!
